title: Crick-Ex One
tagline: A digital cricket scorebook that runs the match, projects the scoreboard, and keeps the data — built in JavaFX with Oracle behind it.
aboutHeadline: A tournament-grade scorebook that doubles as a projector.
blurb: Crick-Ex One is an OOP coursework project taken further than the assignment asked for. It's a full digital cricket scorebook with team and player registration, multi-match management, live ball-by-ball scoring, and a separate full-screen projection mode designed to be thrown on a screen at the side of a real match. State is persisted in Oracle 19c through JDBC, with transaction boundaries drawn so a misclick never corrupts the scoreboard.
pullQuotes: Two UIs, one source of truth — the admin panel and the projection screen stay locked in step.
OOP coursework that ended up running an actual match.
A scorebook that survives the wrong button getting pressed at the wrong time.
highlightsHeadline: Four screens behind a live match.

highlights.title: Teams, players & tournaments
highlights.body: Teams and players are registered through a JavaFX flow backed by Oracle tables, with validation on every field and a searchable filter that scales to a tournament's worth of names. Tournaments group matches; matches group innings; the whole structure is modeled as nested OOP entities with clear ownership.

highlights.title: Matches & calendar
highlights.body: A calendar-driven scheduling layer lets the user place matches across dates, search through historical fixtures, and reopen any past match for replay or correction. The schedule is the single front-door to a tournament's whole history.

highlights.title: Ball-by-ball scoring
highlights.lede: The admin panel where the match actually runs.
highlights.body: An admin-side panel for scoring every ball: runs, wickets, extras, partnerships, bowling figures. Each click writes a transaction to Oracle and pushes an update to the projection screen — so the side-of-the-match display is never more than a ball behind the scorer.

highlights.title: Live scoreboard projection
highlights.body: A second, full-screen JavaFX scene designed to be projected on a screen beside an actual cricket pitch — large type, high contrast, running scoreboard, partnership and bowling figures rotating in. Driven by the same data the admin panel writes; there's no second source.

challenges.title: Keeping the projection and the admin panel in sync
challenges.body: Two JavaFX scenes, one data layer. The fix was to push state through an observable model that both scenes subscribed to — so writes from the admin panel propagated to the projection without an explicit refresh call. The admin and the projected screen never disagreed on the score.

challenges.title: Surviving misclicks during a live match
challenges.body: A live match doesn't pause for an 'are you sure?' dialog. Every scoring action got a one-tap undo (with the previous state cached) and a transactional write to Oracle that could be rolled back. Mistakes stayed cheap; recovery stayed fast.

challenges.title: Modeling cricket's edge cases in OOP
challenges.body: Cricket has a lot of edge cases — retired hurt, no-balls that became free hits, mankad dismissals, super-over tiebreakers. The OOP model leaned on explicit state machines per innings rather than booleans on a Match object, which made adding a rule a matter of adding a state, not patching seven methods.
